evals.prompt: Run a dependency audit on our NestJS backend and React frontend monorepo. We use npm workspaces. Check for vulnerabilities, outdated packages, and license issues. Prioritize findings and give me a remediation plan.
evals.expected_output: npm audit command, triage by severity+reachability, license check, supply chain checks, structured output table, remediation strategies

assertions.name: audit_command
assertions.description: Provides npm audit or equivalent vulnerability scanning command

assertions.name: severity_triage
assertions.description: Triages findings by severity AND reachability, not just CVSS score alone

assertions.name: license_check
assertions.description: Checks license compatibility (flags GPL/AGPL in proprietary code)

assertions.name: supply_chain
assertions.description: Checks for lockfile existence, unpinned deps, suspicious scripts

assertions.name: structured_output
assertions.description: Produces a structured findings table with package, CVE, severity, fix version

assertions.name: remediation_plan
assertions.description: Provides specific remediation strategies (upgrade, override, replace)

evals.prompt: Audit our Django + Celery project (Python 3.11). We use requests, cryptography, Pillow, celery[redis], django-rest-framework, and about 40 other pip packages. Check for CVEs, outdated versions, and license compatibility. We're a commercial SaaS so GPL is a blocker.
evals.expected_output: pip-audit command, checks named packages specifically, severity triage with reachability, GPL license flagging, structured output, remediation per finding

assertions.name: pip_audit_command
assertions.description: Provides pip-audit or safety check command for Python dependencies

assertions.name: checks_named_packages
assertions.description: Specifically mentions checking requests, cryptography, Pillow, celery for known CVEs

assertions.name: severity_triage
assertions.description: Triages by severity and whether the vulnerable code path is actually used

assertions.name: license_flags_gpl
assertions.description: Explicitly flags GPL/AGPL as blockers for commercial SaaS

assertions.name: structured_output
assertions.description: Findings in a table with package, CVE, severity, fix version columns

assertions.name: remediation_per_finding
assertions.description: Specific upgrade commands or replacement suggestions for each finding

evals.prompt: npm audit shows 47 vulnerabilities in our Next.js app — 12 critical, 8 high, 15 moderate, 12 low. Most seem to be in dev dependencies and transitive deps. Help me triage which ones actually matter for production and what to fix first.
evals.expected_output: Separates dev from prod deps, checks reachability, prioritizes critical+reachable, provides specific fix commands, explains transitive vs direct

assertions.name: separates_dev_from_prod
assertions.description: Distinguishes devDependencies (build-time only) from production dependencies

assertions.name: checks_reachability
assertions.description: Checks if the vulnerable code path is actually called in the application

assertions.name: prioritizes_actionable
assertions.description: Focuses on critical+high reachable production deps first, not all 47

assertions.name: provides_fix_commands
assertions.description: Specific npm install or npm overrides commands for each fix

assertions.name: explains_transitive
assertions.description: Explains the difference between direct and transitive dependencies

assertions.name: avoids_force_fix
assertions.description: Does NOT recommend npm audit fix --force blindly as it can break things

evals.prompt: We're evaluating HTTP client libraries for our Node.js backend. Candidates: got, axios, and ky. Before we pick one, audit each for known vulnerabilities, maintenance health (last commit, open issues, bus factor), license, bundle size, and dependency tree depth.
evals.expected_output: Comparative audit of all three packages across security, maintenance, license, size, and deps with a recommendation

assertions.name: audits_all_three
assertions.description: Evaluates got, axios, and ky — not just one

assertions.name: checks_vulnerabilities
assertions.description: Checks CVE history or npm audit advisories for each package

assertions.name: evaluates_maintenance
assertions.description: Checks recent commit activity, issue response time, or contributor count

assertions.name: compares_licenses
assertions.description: Lists the license for each and flags any compatibility issues

assertions.name: evaluates_deps
assertions.description: Compares dependency tree depth or total transitive dependency count

assertions.name: provides_recommendation
assertions.description: Makes a recommendation based on the audit findings

evals.prompt: Our Go microservice (Go 1.22) has about 30 dependencies. Run govulncheck and help us understand the results. We're especially concerned about golang.org/x/crypto, google.golang.org/grpc, and github.com/jackc/pgx.
evals.expected_output: govulncheck command with reachability explanation, checks the 3 named packages specifically, structured output, remediation with go get commands

assertions.name: govulncheck_command
assertions.description: Provides govulncheck ./... command for vulnerability scanning

assertions.name: explains_reachability
assertions.description: Explains that govulncheck shows whether vulnerable code is actually called

assertions.name: checks_named_packages
assertions.description: Specifically addresses golang.org/x/crypto, grpc, and pgx

assertions.name: severity_triage
assertions.description: Triages findings by reachability — reachable vulns are higher priority

assertions.name: structured_output
assertions.description: Findings presented in a structured format with module, CVE, and status

assertions.name: remediation_commands
assertions.description: Provides go get commands to update affected modules
